elements.sent-2411-001: Around the early 2000-s, Bourgain, Katz and Tao introduced an arithmetic approach to study Kakeya-type problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: They showed that the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture follows from a natural problem in additive combinatorics, now referred to as the `Arithmetic Kakeya Conjecture'.
elements.sent-2411-003: We consider a higher dimensional variant of this problem and prove an upper bound using a certain iterative argument.
elements.sent-2411-004: The main new ingredient in our proof is a general way to strengthen the sum-difference inequalities of Katz and Tao which might be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-005: As a corollary, we obtain a new lower bound for the Minkowski dimension of $(n,d)$-Besicovitch sets.
elements.sent-2411-006: The Kakeya problem asks for the smallest possible dimension that a set of points $\mathbb{R ^{n}$ may have if it contains a unit segment in every direction.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Sets with this property are called Besicovitch sets.
elements.sent-2411-008: There are actually multiple questions here, depending on which notion of dimension one considers.
elements.sent-2411-009: The so-called Kakeya conjecture asserts that such sets have full (Hausdorff) dimension for every $n$ and it is related to important questions in harmonic analysis, PDE’s, combinatorics, and number theory \cite{Tao1, Tao2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The conjecture is known in the plane \cite{Davies but widely open for any $n \ge 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: We refer to \cite{KatzZahl, WangZahl2022, WangZahl2024 for recent progress in $\R^3$, and to \cite{Hickman, Zahl for a recent new approach in higher dimensions.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: See \cite{KatzTao for a survey of older results.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The following `Arithmetic Kakeya Conjecture' conjecture, attributed to Katz and Tao \cite{Tao2002 , would imply the Kakeya conjecture for Minkowski dimension (see \cite{Bourgain2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For $r \in \Q$ let $\pi_r: \R^2 \rightarrow \R$ denote the projection $\pi_r(x,y) = x+ry$ and let $\pi_\infty(x,y) = y$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $G \subset \Z^2$ be a finite set such that the map $\pi_\infty$ is injective when restricted on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-017: In \cite{GR , Green and Ruzsa introduced several equivalent formulations of Conjecture \ref{ArithmeticKakeya and in particular they showed that it is equivalent to the following statement about Shannon entropies of integer random variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the setting of arithmetic Kakeya problems, the set-theoretic and entropic languages are completely equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-020: So one can always use the language which is more convenient at any given moment.
elements.sent-2411-021: To make progress on Conjecture \ref{KakeyaEntropy and, thus, on the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture, one may try to make the ``$\varepsilon$'' in (\ref{ak ) as small as possible (while having the flexibility of choosing an arbitrarily large $R$).}}
elements.sent-2411-022: For a finite $R \subset \Q$ define $\beta(R)$ to be the minimum real number $\beta$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:entropy_kakeya} H(Y) \le \beta \max_{r \in R} H(X+r Y)\end{equation} holds for any integer random variables $X, Y$.
elements.sent-2411-023: With this notation, Conjecture \ref{KakeyaEntropy becomes equivalent to the assertion that the limit \begin{equation}\beta_0 = \liminf_{R} \beta(R)\end{equation} taken over all finite $R \subset \Q$ is equal to $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: We refer to \cite{KT1, KT2, Lemm for bounds on $\beta(R)$ for specific choices of $R$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Katz and Tao \cite{KT2 showed that $\beta_0 \le \alpha$ where $\alpha = 1.675\ldots$ is the solution to the equation $\alpha^3-4\alpha+2=0$ and this bound has not been improved for over 20 years.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we are interested in a higher dimensional variant of the arithmetic Kakeya problem.
elements.sent-2411-027: Let $d\ge 1$ and let $R \subset \Q^d$ be a finite set.
elements.sent-2411-028: Then we define $\beta(R)$ as the smallest number such that for any integer random variables $X, Y_1, \ldots, Y_d$ we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:GAPkakeya} H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) \le \beta(R) \max_{(r_1,\ldots,r_d) \in R} H(X + r_1 Y_1 + \ldots + r_d Y_d).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GAPkakeya} H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) \le \beta(R) \max_{(r_1,\ldots,r_d) \in R} H(X + r_1 Y_1 + \ldots + r_d Y_d).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-029: Note that the quantity $\beta(R)$ is well-defined only if $R$ affinely spans $\Q^d$ and that $\beta(\phi(R)) = \beta(R)$ for any affine isomorphism $\phi:\Q^d\rightarrow \Q^d$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The Euclidean counterpart of the problem of estimating $\beta(R)$ for $R \subset \Q^d$ is the Kakeya problem for $d$-dimensional disks (instead of unit segments).
elements.sent-2411-031: Oberlin \cite{Oberlin2 observed that a certain `homogeneous' strengthening of arithmetic Kakeya estimates can be iterated to obtain bounds on Lebesgue measure of $(n,d)$-Besicovitch sets and the related maximal operators.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The combinatorial version of this strengthening can be phrased as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: For a finite set $R \subset \Q$ define $\beta_h(R)$ as the smallest number such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom} H(Y) + (\beta_h(R)-1) H(X|Y) \le \beta_h(R) \max_{r\in R} H(X+r Y)\end{equation} for any integer random variables $X,Y$.
elements.sent-2411-034: So, comparing this to (\ref{eq:entropy_kakeya ), we add an extra term on the left hand side.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Note that in the setting of Conjecture \ref{ArithmeticKakeya one assumes that the mapping $(x,y) \mapsto y$ is injective on $G$, which, in the entropy language, means that the random variable $X$ is determined by the value of $Y$, i.e. $H(X|Y)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: So the extra term in (\ref{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom ) is not immediately useful for the original Kakeya problem.}
elements.sent-2411-037: On the other hand, the fact that (\ref{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom ) is `homogeneous' (i.e. that the sum of coefficients on the left and right hand sides coincide) turns out to be important for the higher dimensional versions of the Kakeya problem.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Oberlin used the `basic iteration' of Katz and Tao \cite{KT2 to (in particular) show that \begin{equation}\label{eq:homogen} \liminf_{R \subset \Q} \beta_h(R) \le 1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\end{equation} holds.}

elements.sent-2411-059: For $n \ge d\ge 1$ say that $K \subset \R^n$ is an {\em $(n, d)$ Besicovitch set if for any $d$-dimensional subspace $U \subset \R^n$ there is a translate $(x+U \cap B^n(0,1)) \subset K$.}

elements.sent-2411-073: Let $X$ be a discrete random variable, the Shannon entropy $H(X)$ is defined by the formula \begin{equation}H(X) := \sum_{x} \Pr(X = x) \log \frac{1}{\Pr(X=x)}\end{equation} where the summation is over all the elements $x$ in the range of $X$ for which $\Pr(X=x)$ is nonzero.
elements.sent-2411-074: Given two random variables $X$ and $Y$, define the conditional entropy as \begin{equation}H(X|Y) := \sum_{y} \Pr(Y=y)H(X|Y=y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: We have the following chain rule for the entropy of the joint random variable $(X,Y)$: \begin{equation}H(X,Y) = H(X|Y) + H(Y) = H(Y|X) + H(X).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: More generally, for any choice of discrete random variables $X_{1 ,\ldots,X_{n , Z$, we have the conditional chain rule: \begin{equation}H(X_{1},\ldots,X_{n} | Z) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H(X_i | X_1,\ldots,X_{i-1},Z).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Another basic property is that conditioning on a random variable cannot increase entropy: \begin{equation}H(X|Y) \leq H(X).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-078: An important consequence of this result is the submodularity of entropy: \begin{equation}H(X,Y|Z) \leq H(X|Z) + H(Y|Z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, we will need the Shearer’s inequality.
elements.sent-2411-080: Here and throughout we use $[n]$ for $\left\{1,\ldots,n\right\}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: For a random vector $(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$, \begin{equation}H(X_1,\ldots,X_n) \leq \frac{1}{t} \sum_{F \in F} H(X_{F}),\end{equation} where $X_{F}$ is the vector $(X_i: i \in F)$.

elements.sent-2411-144: We start with an arbitrary set $R \subset \Q$ and a pair of random variables $X,Y$.
elements.sent-2411-146: We are going to show the estimate \begin{equation}\label{eq:goal} (1-\varepsilon_0) H(Y) + (\beta(R)-1) H(X|Y) \le \beta(R) \max_{r \in R} H(X+rY).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-147: Taking $\varepsilon_0$ to approach zero would then complete the proof.
elements.sent-2411-148: We may assume that $H(Y) \ge (1+\varepsilon_0) \max_{r \in R H(X+rY)$ for any $r \in R$ since otherwise (\ref{eq:goal ) holds automatically.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: By replacing $X$ with $m X$ and $R$ with $m R$ for a constant $m = m(R)$ depending on $R$, we may assume that $R \subset \Z$ (let $m$ to be the product of all denominators of all $r \in R$).
elements.sent-2411-150: Thus, we may assume that $R \subset [-k, k] \cap \Z$ for some constant $k$ depending on $R$.
elements.sent-2411-151: We perform a sequence of reductions.
elements.sent-2411-152: Apply Lemma \ref{lem:ruzsa to the pair $(X, Y)$, the set $R$ and some parameter $\varepsilon \ll \varepsilon_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Next, apply Lemma \ref{lem:freiman to the graph $G$ to obtain a new graph $G' \subset \F_p^2$ with the following properties:.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Let $\alpha = \varepsilon_1 + \max_{r \in R \frac{H(X+rY) {H(Y)}$ and $\gamma = \frac{H(X|Y) {H(Y) - \varepsilon_1$, where $\varepsilon_1=c\varepsilon_0$ for a sufficiently small constant $c>0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Let $A = \bigcup_{r \in R \pi_r(G')$ then we get an upper bound $|A| \le p^{\alpha-\varepsilon}$ provided that $\varepsilon$ is small enough compared to $\varepsilon_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Let $B = \pi_\infty(G')$, by the above, we have $|B| \sim p$.
elements.sent-2411-158: For $y \in B$ let $D_y = \{x:~ (x, y) \in G'\}$ be the fiber above $y$.
elements.sent-2411-159: We have for any $y\in B$ \begin{equation}|D_y| \ge 2^{(1\pm \varepsilon) H(X|Y) n} \ge p^{\gamma+\varepsilon}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-160: Let $m = p^{1-\gamma}$, sample uniformly random elements $t \in \F_p$ and $d \in \F_p\setminus\{0\}$ and define random arithmetic progressions \begin{equation}I_0 = \{d j, ~j = 0, \ldots, m-1\}, I = I_0+t = \{t + d j, ~j = 0, \ldots, m-1\}, J = \{d j, ~ j \in [m/2, m] \},\end{equation} and let us also put $\widetilde I = I+k J -kJ$ which is an arithmetic progression of length at most $2k m$ (recall that $k$ was chosen so that $R \subset [-k,k]$).
elements.formula-2411-044: \begin{equation}I_0 = \{d j, ~j = 0, \ldots, m-1\}, I = I_0+t = \{t + d j, ~j = 0, \ldots, m-1\}, J = \{d j, ~ j \in [m/2, m] \},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-161: Now we define a random set \begin{equation}G_I' = \{(x, y) \in G':~ x \in I, ~ y \in J \}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-045: \begin{equation}G_I' = \{(x, y) \in G':~ x \in I, ~ y \in J \}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-162: Observe that if $(x, y) \in G_I'$ then for $r\in R$, \begin{equation}x+r y \in I + r J \subset \widetilde I,\end{equation} and so we get $\pi_r(G_I') \subset \widetilde I \cap A$.
elements.sent-2411-163: The size of the latter set can be easily controlled by Markov's inequality: \begin{equation}\label{eq:markov} \Pr\left[|\widetilde I \cap A| \ge T \frac{m}{p} |A|\right] \lesssim k T^{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-164: Let us now understand the projection $B_I =\pi_\infty(G_I')$.
elements.sent-2411-165: By definition $y \in B_I$ if and only if $y \in J$ and $D_y \cap I \neq \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-166: Note that $y\in J$ means that $d = y/j$ for some $j \in [m/2, m]$.
elements.sent-2411-167: Conditioned on $d = y/j$, we have $D_y \cap I \neq \emptyset $ if and only if \begin{equation}t \in D_y - (y/j) I_0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: So we conclude that \begin{equation}\label{eq:pry} \Pr[y \in B_I] = \sum_{j \in [m/2, m]} \frac{|D_y- (y/j)I_0|}{p(p-1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: To lower bound this quantity, we apply Lemma \ref{lem_fou to the set $A= -y^{-1 D_y$ and $\varepsilon>0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-170: By construction we have $|D_y| \ge p/m$ and so by Lemma \ref{lem_fou , for at least $[m p^{-\varepsilon ]$ indices $j \in [m/2, m]$ we have $|D_y- (y/j)I_0| \ge p^{1-\varepsilon}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: So in (\ref{eq:pry ) we get \begin{equation}\Pr[y \in B_I] \ge [m p^{-\varepsilon}] p^{-1-\varepsilon}\end{equation} Recall that $m = p^{1-\gamma}$, $\gamma = \frac{H(X|Y) {H(Y) -\varepsilon_1$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-181: Then \begin{equation}H(Y') = \log |\pi_\infty(G_I)| \ge \log m - 2\varepsilon \log p = (1-\gamma -2\varepsilon) \log p\end{equation} \begin{equation}H(X'+rY') \le \log |\pi_r(G_I)| \le (3\varepsilon-1)\log p +\log m + \log |A| \le (\alpha-\gamma +3\varepsilon) \log p\end{equation} and so by (\ref{eq:entropy_kakeya ) we obtain \begin{equation}H(Y') \le \beta(R) \max_{r\in R} H(X'+rY'),\end{equation} \begin{equation}1-\gamma -2\varepsilon \le \beta(R) (\alpha-\gamma +3\varepsilon),\end{equation} recall that $\gamma = \frac{H(X|Y) {H(Y) -\varepsilon_1$ and $\alpha = \max_{r\in R \frac{H(X+rY) {H(Y) +\varepsilon_1$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-183: Taking $\varepsilon_0 \rightarrow 0$ then recovers the homogeneous Kakeya inequality (\ref{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom ).}
elements.sent-2411-184: In this section we prove non-trivial bounds on $\beta(R)$ for $R \subset \Q^d$ in terms of the original arithmetic Kakeya inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-185: For $d\ge 1$ and $R \subset \Q^d$ let us denote $\delta(R) = \frac{\beta(R) / d {\beta(R)/d-1}$, this quantity makes for cleaner statement of the result below.}
elements.sent-2411-187: We want to prove an upper bound on $H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d)$ in terms of $H(X+r_1Y_1+\ldots+r_dY_d)$ for $(r_1, \ldots,r_d) \in R = R_1\times \ldots \times R_d$.. We expand \begin{equation}H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) = \sum_{i=1}^d H(Y_i | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Fix an index $i \in \{1,\ldots, d\}$ and fix $r_{i+1 \in R_{i+1 , \ldots, r_d \in R_d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: We apply (\ref{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom ) to the variables \begin{equation}\tilde X = X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}, \tilde Y = Y_{i} | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-059: \begin{equation}\tilde X = X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}, \tilde Y = Y_{i} | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-190: We obtain \begin{equation}H(\tilde Y) + (\beta(R_i)-1) H(\tilde X | \tilde Y) \le \beta(R_i) \max_{r_i \in R_i} H(\tilde X + r_i \tilde Y)\end{equation} which by expanding and denoting $\beta_i = \beta(R_i)$ gives \begin{equation}\label{eq3} H(Y_i|Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}) + (\beta_i-1) H(X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_i) \le\end{equation} \begin{equation}\le \beta_i \max_{r_i \in R_i} H(X+r_{i} Y_{i} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-060: \begin{equation}H(\tilde Y) + (\beta(R_i)-1) H(\tilde X | \tilde Y) \le \beta(R_i) \max_{r_i \in R_i} H(\tilde X + r_i \tilde Y)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-061: \begin{equation}\label{eq3} H(Y_i|Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}) + (\beta_i-1) H(X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_i) \le\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-062: \begin{equation}\le \beta_i \max_{r_i \in R_i} H(X+r_{i} Y_{i} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-191: Let \begin{equation}H_i = \max_{r_{i+1} \in R_{i+1}, \ldots, r_d\in R_d} H(X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i}),\end{equation} then (\ref{eq3 ) gives \begin{equation}\label{eq4} H(Y_i|Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}) \le \beta_i H_{i-1} - (\beta_i-1) H_i.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}H_i = \max_{r_{i+1} \in R_{i+1}, \ldots, r_d\in R_d} H(X+r_{i+1} Y_{i+1} + \ldots + r_d Y_d | Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-192: Let $\delta_i = \frac{\beta_i {\beta_i-1}$, multiply both sides of (\ref{eq4 ) by $\frac{1 {\delta_1 \ldots \delta_{i-1}\beta_i}$ and sum over $i=1, \ldots, d$, we get: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^d \frac{1}{\delta_1 \ldots \delta_{i-1}\beta_i} H(Y_i|Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1}) \le \sum_{i=1}^d \frac{\beta_i H_{i-1} - (\beta_i-1) H_i}{\delta_1 \ldots \delta_{i-1} \beta_i} = H_0 - \frac{H_d}{\delta_1\ldots \delta_d}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: The sums of coefficients on both sides are the same since this is true for all instances of (\ref{eq:entropy-kakeya-hom ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: If we expand $H(Y_i|Y_1, \ldots,Y_{i-1 ) = H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_i) - H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i-1 )$ and group same terms then one can check that the coefficient of $H(Y_1,\ldots, Y_i)$ in the resulting expression is equal to $\frac{\beta_{i+1 -\beta_i+1 {\delta_1\ldots\delta_{i-1}\beta_i\beta_{i+1 }$ which is a positive number since $\beta(R) \in (1,2]$ for any $R \subset \Q$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Now we can average this bound over all permutations of $Y_1, \ldots, Y_d$, apply Shearer's inequality (Lemma \ref{lem:shearer ) \begin{equation}H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) \le \frac{d}{k} \E_{i_1<\ldots< i_k} H(Y_{i_1}, \ldots, Y_{i_k})\end{equation} and conclude that \begin{equation}\left(1-\frac{1}{\delta_1\ldots \delta_d}\right) \frac{1}{d} H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) \le H_0 - \frac{H_d}{\delta_1\ldots \delta_d}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\left(1-\frac{1}{\delta_1\ldots \delta_d}\right) \frac{1}{d} H(Y_1, \ldots, Y_d) + \frac{1}{\delta_1\ldots\delta_d} H(X|Y_1,\ldots Y_d) \le \max_{r_i\in R_i} H(X+r_1Y_1+\ldots r_dY_d)\end{equation} which after rearrangement gives us the desired bound.}
